If a person for instance, were to have a day of Ramadan to finish, to make up, right? And you know, their day off happens to be a Saturday or happens to be a Friday. Would it be permissible for that person to make that day up on that day? Yes, because it's not an optional fasting, it's an obligatory fasting, right? Okay, the fifth one that the author mentions here is an-nisf min Sha'ban The second half of Sha'ban for a person who isn't who isn't accustomed to fasting at a particular pattern. So, the second half of Sha'ban. Sha'ban is the eighth month. And it is the month just before Ramadan. 15 days before Ramadan or 14 days, once a person hits the 15th of Sha'ban, it becomes impermissible for a person to fast unless they are accustomed to fasting on a particular schedule. Let's say someone usually fasts Mondays and Thursdays. Or a person usually fasts you know, every other day. Or a person usually fasts This person can continue his fast. The only one who is prohibited or the optional fasting which is prohibited at the second half of Shaban is the one who is trying to gear up for Ramadan. They don't have a particular schedule that they adhere to. It's just that Ramadan is approaching so they begin to fast early. This is impermissible. It is not permissible to fast after the second or during the second half of Shaban. He says on the authority of Abu Hurairah who narrates that the messenger of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala said if the second half of Shaban commences, then do not fast. If the second half of Shaban commences, do not fast. Now, this narration is more akin to the next prohibition, or the next prohibited time of fasting. So, we have the second half of Sha'ban. Yawm al-Shakk is when we are looking for the moon for Ramadan. We are looking for the crescent for Ramadan. This is a prohibited time of fasting, and this is positively prohibited in the following narration. Abu al-Qasim is the prophet sal Allah who alayhi wasallam. That was his kunya. So, here Ammar ibn Yasir is telling us that if a person fasts on that day in which the moon has not been sighted, but it could have likely been sighted, but we're unsure, there is shak there. When it becomes publicly acknowledged, that is the month. The month is the acknowledgement of the majority of people, not the crescent. The crescent is not the month. Okay? It's taken from that same root meaning, which is something becoming well-known, becoming public, becoming accepted, becoming widely acknowledged. This is called a shahada. It doesn't matter if the crescent was there or not. As a matter of fact, it doesn't matter if the crescent was seen by even one person unless we have widespread acknowledgement of the month or 30 days have been completed of the previous month the month has not started yet. So, Ramadan is actually happening somewhere, but we have no idea that it's happening. This doesn't exist. This is a hypothetical that is non-existent. Therefore, the prophet Sallallahu alaihi wasallam positively prohibited that a person fast during what? During the day that is referred to as Yawm ash-Shakk. summary: This session continues the study of Al-Wajeez, focusing on the chapter regarding prohibited days for optional fasting. The instructor reviews five previously mentioned prohibitions (1st of Shawwal, 10th of Dhu al-Hijjah, days of Tashriq, Friday, and Saturday) and introduces two additional prohibitions: fasting during the second half of Sha'ban and fasting on Yawm al-Shakk (the day of doubt). The lesson emphasizes that these prohibitions apply only to optional fasting, not obligatory fasting like making up missed Ramadan days. A significant portion of the lecture clarifies the Islamic ruling on moon sighting and the determination of the Islamic month, explaining that the month is established through public acknowledgement or the completion of 29-30 days, not merely by sighting the crescent.
suggested_title: Al-Wajeez: Prohibited Days for Optional Fasting and Moon Sighting Rulings
hadith_chapter: Book of Fasting - Chapter on Days Prohibited for Fasting
catchy_title: Which Days Can't You Fast? Islamic Rulings Explained
